An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together. </p><p>To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.</p><p>The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="297e311a-a71b-4025-8383-99f459a353d3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Operational Parameter</li><li>Standard Operational Activity</li><li>Node</li><li>Is Capable Of Performing</li><li>Operational Activity Action</li><li>Operational Activity Edge</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li><li>Energy</li><li>Materiel</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3bfa42f4-f543-470e-aa1f-786f99cdedf9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Model diagram</li><li>Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Flow Model diagram</li><li>Displaying possible Operational Exchanges on the selected Operational Activity Edge</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="28d6248e-d5e0-43e2-beb4-b99cb2d5bd6c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Exchange Creation Wizard</li><li>Operational Exchange Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575368 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01589: MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model

- page_id: `249575368`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575368/MODAF.+OV-6a+Operational+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.775+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Rules Model specifies operational or business rules that are constraints on an enterprise, a mission, operation, business, or an architecture. While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed 
rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.

**Implementation**

OV-6a can be represented using:

- An OV-6a table.
- An OV-6a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6a Operational Rules Model

**Related views**

An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.

As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Constraint
- Node
- Operational Activity
- Entity Item
- Operational Exchange
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-6a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed<br />rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6a can be represented using:</p><ul><li>An OV-6a table.</li><li>An OV-6a spreadsheet report.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6a.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6a Operational Rules Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.</p><p>As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58c52e31-42e9-409b-bab5-8ad8152b9b6d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Operational Constraint</li><li>Node</li><li>Operational Activity</li><li>Entity Item</li><li>Operational Exchange</li><li>Exchange Element</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f51a094-1bff-4ab7-a695-e2941c994888"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating OV-6a table</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575370 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01590: MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

- page_id: `249575370`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575370/MODAF.+OV-6b+Operational+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.802+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.

Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.

**Implementation**

OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

**Related views**

An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational State Description
- Operational State
- Node

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575372 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01591: MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `249575372`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575372/MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.828+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Operational Event Trace
- Operational Message
- Operational Exchange
- Node
- Node Role

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Operational Exchange Creation Wizard
- Operational Exchange Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.</p><p>The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.</li><li>Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6c.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575374 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01592: MODAF. OV-7 Information Model

- page_id: `249575374`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575374/MODAF.+OV-7+Information+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.853+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Logical Data Model describes the structure of an architecture domain’s system data types and the structural business process rules (defined in the architecture’s Operational View) that govern the system data. It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data 
models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.

In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

OV-7 can be represented using:

- An OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-7 Information Model

**Related views**

An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.

Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Logical Data Model
- Entity Item
- Entity Attribute
- Entity Relationship
- Details
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating OV-7 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575377 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01593: MODAF. SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

- page_id: `249575377`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575377/MODAF.+SOV-1+Service+Taxonomy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.892+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Service Taxonomy View (SOV-1) specifies a hierarchy of services. The elements in the hierarchy are service specifications (i.e. service interfaces), and the relationships between the elements are specializations – i.e. one Service is a special type of another. Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.

**Implementation**

SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

**Related views**

The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Capability
- Expose

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SOV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575379 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01594: MODAF. SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

- page_id: `249575379`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575379/MODAF.+SOV-2+Service+Interface+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.920+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interface Specification View (SOV-2) is to define the interfaces presented by a service. A Service presents one or more interfaces to consumers (a “consumer” being any agent capable of using the service – a person, an organization, a system or another service). In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.

**Implementation**

SOV-2 can be represented using

- A report which is automatically generated from all data
- A SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Service Operation
- Service Attribute
- Service Parameter
- Service
- Request

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-2 can be represented using</p><ul><li>A report which is automatically generated from all data</li><li>A SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575381 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01595: MODAF. SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

- page_id: `249575381`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575381/MODAF.+SOV-3+Capability+to+Service+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.947+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Capability to Service Mapping View (SOV-3) depicts which services contribute to the achievement of a capability. It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely couple services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is important to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.

**Implementation**

SOV-3 can be represented using a StV-6 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interface
- Expose
- Capability

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Building SOV-3 matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575383 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01596: MODAF. SOV-4a Service Constraints

- page_id: `249575383`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575383/MODAF.+SOV-4a+Service+Constraints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.975+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Constraints View (SOV-4a) is to specify constraints that apply to providers and consumers of services. To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. An SOV-4a product specifies constraints against services to which implementations of the service must conform.

**Implementation**

SOV-4a can be represented using:

- A SOV-4a table.
- A SOV-4a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4a Service Constraints

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Policy
- Service Interface

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Filling in SOV-4a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575385 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01597: MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model

- page_id: `249575385`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575385/MODAF.+SOV-4b+Service+State+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.008+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service State Model View (SOV-4b) is to specify the possible states a service may have, and the possible transitions between those states. It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.

As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.

**Implementation**

SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4b Service State Model

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service State Machine
- Service Interface

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The purpose of the Service State Model View (SOV-4b) is to specify the possible states a service may have, and the possible transitions between those states. It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.</p><p>As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-4b Service State Model</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="75c54b3f-47ae-41ff-ad8e-2550694c2933"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Service State Machine</li><li>Service Interface</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575387 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01598: MODAF. SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

- page_id: `249575387`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575387/MODAF.+SOV-4c+Service+Interaction+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.038+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interaction Specification View (SOV-4c) is to specify how a service interacts with external agents, and the sequence and dependencies of those interactions. An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.

**Implementation**

SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Interaction
- Service Message
- Service Interface
- Service Attribute

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4c.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575389 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01599: MODAF. SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

- page_id: `249575389`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575389/MODAF.+SOV-5+Service+Functionality+Flow
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.067+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Service Functionality View (SOV-5) defines the behavior of a service in terms of the functions it is expected to perform. SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. Equivalent in nature to OV-5 and SV-4, it specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform. It is especially useful during the initial exploration of the requirements for a service.

**Implementation**

SOV-5 can be represented using:

- A SOV-5 diagram for the Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- A SOV-5 diagram based on the UML Clas diagram.
- A UML Activity diagram.
- A SysML Activity diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Service Functionality Description

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

**Related views**

An SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. It is equivalent in nature to the OV-5 and SV-4. It specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform.

An SOV-5 specifies the required functionality that an implementation of a service is expected to have – the implementation is represented in the SV-1 and SV-4.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Service Function
- Service Operation Action
- Service Function Action
- Function Edge
- Service Operation
- Service Parameter
- Service Interface

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Description diagram
- Creating SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Service Functionality View (SOV-5) defines the behavior of a service in terms of the functions it is expected to perform. SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. Equivalent in nature to OV-5 and SV-4, it specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform. It is especially useful during the initial exploration of the requirements for a service.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-5 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>A SOV-5 diagram for the Operational Activity flows. SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Service Functionality Description</h6><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_5_example.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575333 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01600: MODAF. StV-1 Enterprise Vision

- page_id: `249575333`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575333/MODAF.+StV-1+Enterprise+Vision
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.378+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

StV-1 addresses the enterprise concerns associated with the overall vision for transformational endeavors and thus defines the strategic context for a group of Enterprise capabilities. The purpose of an StV-1 is to provide a strategic context for the capabilities described in the Architecture. It also provides a high-level scope for the Architecture that is more general than the scenario-based scope defined in an OV-1. The Views are high-level and describe capabilities using terminology that is easily understood by non-technical readers (though they may make extensive use of military terminology and acronyms that are clearly defined in the AV-2 View).

**Implementation**

StV-1 can be represented using a StV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-1 Enterprise Vision

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Enterprise Goal
- Enterprise Vision
- Enterprise Phase
- Whole-Life Enterprise
- Structural Part
- Temporal Part
- Enduring Task
- Capability
- Exhibits

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- Creating StV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575335 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01601: MODAF. StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

- page_id: `249575335`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575335/MODAF.+StV-2+Capability+Taxonomy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.402+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The view presents a hierarchy of capabilities. These capabilities may be presented in context of an Enterprise Phase, i.e. it can show the required capabilities for current and future enterprises. The StV-2 specifies all the capabilities that are reference throughout one or more architectures. In addition it can be used as a source document for the development of high-level use cases and Key User Requirements (KUR). The StV-2 also provides metrics against each capability that may be used to measure successfully fielded capability.

**Implementation**

StV-2 can be represented using a StV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, the old StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Exhibits
- Climate
- Environment
- Environment Property
- Light Condition
- Location

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-2 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The StV-2 specifies all the capabilities that are reference throughout one or more architectures. In addition it can be used as a source document for the development of high-level use cases and Key User Requirements (KUR). The StV-2 also provides metrics against each capability that may be used to measure successfully fielded capability.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-2 can be represented using a StV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d21ac2a-4f03-4e37-b560-599e74de7180"><ac:rich-text-body>If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, the old StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. StV-2 Capability Taxonomy" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>StV-2 Capability Taxonomy</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f55e24b7-86e7-4216-bb18-32fc09b69095"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Capability</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Climate</li><li>Environment</li><li>Environment Property</li><li>Light Condition</li><li>Location</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="469e623f-b0c9-49bd-b21e-c4bd976a28ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating StV-2 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575337 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01602: MODAF. StV-3 Capability Phasing

- page_id: `249575337`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575337/MODAF.+StV-3+Capability+Phasing
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.424+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

StV-3 addresses the planned achievement of capability at different points in time or during specific periods of time, i.e. capability phasing. StV-3 Views support the Capability Audit process by providing a method to identify gaps or duplication in capability provision. The view indicates capability increments, which should be associated with delivery milestones within acquisition projects (when the increments are associated with capability deliveries).

**Implementation**

StV-3 can be represented using a StV-3 diagram which is realized as a time based diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-3 Capability Phasing

**Related views**

The View is created by analyzing programmatic project data to determine when the projects providing elements of military capability are to be delivered, upgraded and/or withdrawn (this data may be provided in part by a Programme Timelines (AcV-2) View). Then the capability increments identified are structured according to the required capabilities determined in the Capability Taxonomy (StV-2) View and the Enterprise Phases (from StV-1).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Related elements
- Capability
- Capability Configuration
- Exhibits
- Increment Milestone
- Out Of Service Milestone
- Actual Project

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>StV-3 addresses the planned achievement of capability at different points in time or during specific periods of time, i.e. capability phasing. StV-3 Views support the Capability Audit process by providing a method to identify gaps or duplication in capability provision. The view indicates capability increments, which should be associated with delivery milestones within acquisition projects (when the increments are associated with capability deliveries).</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-3 can be represented using a StV-3 diagram which is realized as a time based diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. StV-3 Capability Phasing" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>StV-3 Capability Phasing</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The View is created by analyzing programmatic project data to determine when the projects providing elements of military capability are to be delivered, upgraded and/or withdrawn (this data may be provided in part by a Programme Timelines (AcV-2) View). Then the capability increments identified are structured according to the required capabilities determined in the Capability Taxonomy (StV-2) View and the Enterprise Phases (from StV-1).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07ea6e2c-5649-4b56-b918-a7ae28dfc204"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Related elements</li><li>Capability</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Increment Milestone</li><li>Out Of Service Milestone</li><li>Actual Project</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575339 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01603: MODAF. StV-4 Capability Dependencies

- page_id: `249575339`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575339/MODAF.+StV-4+Capability+Dependencies
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.445+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

An StV-4 Product describes the dependencies between planned capabilities. It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. The StV-4 View provides a means of analyzing the dependencies between capabilities. The groupings of capabilities are logical, and the purpose of the groupings is to guide enterprise management.

**Implementation**

StV-4 can be represented using a StV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-4 Capability Dependencies

**Related views**

The Capability Dependencies (StV-4) View describes the relationships between capabilities (capabilities may be reused from an StV-2). It also defines logical groupings of capabilities. This contrasts with the StV-2 which also deals with relationships between Capabilities, but the StV-2 only addresses the specialization-generalization relationship (i.e. capability taxonomy).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Capability Property

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-4 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The groupings of capabilities are logical, and the purpose of the groupings is to guide enterprise management.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>StV-4 can be represented using a StV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_4.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. This contrasts with the StV-2 which also deals with relationships between Capabilities, but the StV-2 only addresses the specialization-generalization relationship (i.e. capability taxonomy).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="847260cc-d8c2-4ec6-8247-6debd84712ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Capability</li><li>Capability Property</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00449095-7638-47e9-8c65-583f4959fca0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating StV-4 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575341 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01604: MODAF. StV-5 Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping

- page_id: `249575341`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575341/MODAF.+StV-5+Capability+to+Organization+Deployment+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.466+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The StV-5 defines Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping. It addresses the fulfillment of capability requirements, in particular by network enabled capabilities. This view shows the planned capability deployment and interconnection for a particular EnterprisePhase. This view will provide a more detailed dependency analysis than is possible using StV-3. The StV-5 View is used to support the capability management process and, in particular, assist the planning of fielding.

**Implementation**

StV-5 can be represented using:

- A StV-5 table. 
 If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, theold StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.
- A StV-5 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-5 Capability to Organization Deployment Mapping

**Related views**

| Icon | View | Relationship |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | StV-1 Enterprise Vision | Capability Phasing is defined in StV-1. |
|  | StV-2 Capability Taxonomy | Capabilities shown in StV-5 table are usually modeled in StV-2 Capability Taxonomy. |
|  | AcV-2 Programme Timelines | The timing is based on project milestones from AcV2 which indicate when capability configuration will bedelivered to an organiation. |
|  | OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart | Actual Organizational Resources related to deployment of Capability Configurations are taken from OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart. |

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Capability Configuration
- Exhibits
- Actual Organization
- Actual Post
- Deployed Milestone
- No Longer Used Milestone
- Actual Project
- Enterprise Phase
- Whole-Life Enterprise

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating StV-5 table
- Modifying StV-5 table

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Deployment Milestones Creation Wizard

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575347 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01605: MODAF. StV-6 Operational Activity to Capability Mapping

- page_id: `249575347`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575347/MODAF.+StV-6+Operational+Activity+to+Capability+Mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:14.531+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The StV-6 describes the mapping between the capabilities required by an Enterprise and the operational activities that those capabilities support.

**Implementation**

StV-6 can be represented using a StV-6 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Capabilities will be used as row elements and the Operational Activities will be used as column elements.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-6 Operational Activity to Capability Mapping

**Related views**

The StV-6 View provides a bridge between capability analyses using StVs and operational activities analyzed using OVs. Specifically, it identifies how operational activities can be performed using various available capability elements. It is similar in function to the SV-5 which maps system functions to operational activities.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Capability
- Standard Operational Activity
- Maps to Capability

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Building StV-6 matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The Capabilities will be used as row elements and the Operational Activities will be used as column elements.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stv_6.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. It is similar in function to the SV-5 which maps system functions to operational activities.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9539cd55-012a-4ebc-a485-6c717edc6876"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Capability</li><li>Standard Operational Activity</li><li>Maps to Capability</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95697d58-78a8-4dfa-925a-147e92cef80f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Building StV-6 matrix</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /><br /><br /><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575393 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01606: MODAF. SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification

- page_id: `249575393`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575393/MODAF.+SV-1+Resource+Interaction+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.128+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Resource Interaction Specification (SV-1) addresses the composition and interaction of resources. SV-1 now incorporates the human elements – Posts, Organizations and Roles. This view was previously known as the System Interface Description; the name change reflects the expanded scope of modeling in the solution space. The Resource Interaction Specification (SV-1) links together the operational and systems architecture views by depicting how resources are structured and interact in order to realize the logical architecture specified in an OV-2. An SV-1 may represent the realization of a requirement specified in an OV-2 (i.e. in a to-be architecture), and so there may be many alternative SV configurations that could realize the operational requirement. Alternatively, in an as-is architecture, the OV-2 may simply be a simplified, logical representation of the SV-1 to allow communication of key information flows to non-technical stakeholders. A resource interaction is a simplified representation of a pathway or network, usually depicted graphically as a connector (i.e. a line with possible amplifying information). The SV-1 depicts all interactions between resources that are of interest to the architect.

Note that interactions between systems may be further specified in detail in the SV-2 and SV-6. Sub-resource assemblies may be identified in the SV-1 to any level (i.e. depth) of decomposition the architect sees fit. The SV-1 may also identify the Physical Assets (e.g. Platforms) at which resources are deployed, and optionally overlay Operational Nodes that utilize those resources. In many cases, an operational node depicted in an OV-2 product may well be the logical representation of the resource that is shown in the SV-1.

**Implementation**

SV-1 can be represented using:

- A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification

**Related views**

An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with nodes originally specified in an OV-2. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.

An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one resource to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an SV-2. Resource Interactions are summarized in a Resource Interactions Matrix (SV-3).

The functions performed by the resources are specified in an SV-4 Resource Functionality Description, but may optionally be overlaid on the Resources in the SV-1.

An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As OV-2 and OV-5 specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and SV-4 specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Resource Role
- Resource Interface
- Resource Port
- Resource Connector
- Capability
- Exhibits
- Fielded Capability
- Service
- Request
- Resource Interaction
- Control
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SV-1 diagram
- Creating Resource Interaction in SV-1 diagram

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Resource Interaction Creation Wizard
- Resource Interaction Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Resource Interaction Specification (SV-1) addresses the composition and interaction of resources. SV-1 now incorporates the human elements – Posts, Organizations and Roles. This view was previously known as the System Interface Description; the name change reflects the expanded scope of modeling in the solution space. The Resource Interaction Specification (SV-1) links together the operational and systems architecture views by depicting how resources are structured and interact in order to realize the logical architecture specified in an OV-2. An SV-1 may represent the realization of a requirement specified in an OV-2 (i.e. in a to-be architecture), and so there may be many alternative SV configurations that could realize the operational requirement. Alternatively, in an as-is architecture, the OV-2 may simply be a simplified, logical representation of the SV-1 to allow communication of key information flows to non-technical stakeholders. A resource interaction is a simplified representation of a pathway or network, usually depicted graphically as a connector (i.e. a line with possible amplifying information). The SV-1 depicts all interactions between resources that are of interest to the architect. </p><p>Note that interactions between systems may be further specified in detail in the SV-2 and SV-6. Sub-resource assemblies may be identified in the SV-1 to any level (i.e. depth) of decomposition the architect sees fit. The SV-1 may also identify the Physical Assets (e.g. Platforms) at which resources are deployed, and optionally overlay Operational Nodes that utilize those resources. In many cases, an operational node depicted in an OV-2 product may well be the logical representation of the resource that is shown in the SV-1.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A UML Class diagram.</li><li>A UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>A SysML Block Definition diagram.</li><li>A SysML Internal Block diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sv_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with nodes originally specified in an OV-2. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.</p><p>An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one resource to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an SV-2. Resource Interactions are summarized in a Resource Interactions Matrix (SV-3).</p><p>The functions performed by the resources are specified in an SV-4 Resource Functionality Description, but may optionally be overlaid on the Resources in the SV-1.</p><p>An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As OV-2 and OV-5 specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and SV-4 specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0e83c577-e5b8-43b6-b6d6-0003cf416836"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Resource Artifact</li><li>Software</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Organization</li><li>Post</li><li>Resource Role</li><li>Resource Interface</li><li>Resource Port</li><li>Resource Connector</li><li>Capability</li><li>Exhibits</li><li>Fielded Capability</li><li>Service</li><li>Request</li><li>Resource Interaction</li><li>Control</li><li>Exchange Element</li><li>Energy</li><li>Materiel</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61f677a6-546d-413f-8b00-a3db92770ac9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating SV-1 diagram</li><li>Creating Resource Interaction in SV-1 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e56a9292-c5dc-48f9-a890-5d8bfa5b1c27"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related references</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Resource Interaction Creation Wizard</li><li>Resource Interaction Manager Dialog</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575395 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01607: MODAF. SV-1 Resource Internal Interaction Specification

- page_id: `249575395`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575395/MODAF.+SV-1+Resource+Internal+Interaction+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.160+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Resource Internal Interaction Specification (SV-1) addresses the internal composition and interaction of resources.

**Implementation**

SV-1 can be represented using:

- A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A SV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-1 Resource Internal Interaction Specification

**Related views**

An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with nodes originally specified in an OV-2. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.

An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one resource to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an SV-2. Resource Interactions are summarized in a Resource Interactions Matrix (SV-3).

The functions performed by the resources are specified in an SV-4 Resource Functionality Description, but may optionally be overlaid on the Resources in the SV-1.

An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As OV-2 and OV-5 specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and SV-4 specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- System
- Resource Interface
- Resource Port
- Resource Connector
- Service
- Request
- Resource Interaction

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SV-1 diagram
- Creating Resource Interaction in SV-1 diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

SV-1 Resource Internal Interaction Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SV-1 Resource Internal Interaction Specification</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An SV-1 can optionally be adorned with nodes originally specified in an OV-2. In this way, traceability can be established from the logical OV structure to the physical SV structure.</p><p>An interaction, as depicted in the SV-1, is an indicator that information passes from one resource to another. In the case of systems, this can be expanded into further detail in an SV-2. Resource Interactions are summarized in a Resource Interactions Matrix (SV-3).</p><p>The functions performed by the resources are specified in an SV-4 Resource Functionality Description, but may optionally be overlaid on the Resources in the SV-1.</p><p>An Operational View (OV) suite may specify a set of requirements – either as a specific operational plan, or a scenario for procurement. As OV-2 and OV-5 specify the logical structure and behavior, SV-1 and SV-4 specify the physical structure and behavior (to the level of detail required by the architectural stakeholders).</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b711eee-3b57-4aef-9130-7f1b0912fe4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>System</li><li>Resource Interface</li><li>Resource Port</li><li>Resource Connector</li><li>Service</li><li>Request</li><li>Resource Interaction</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d5ed8ac3-b7d7-4e3c-871b-6f80a3936f20"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Creating SV-1 diagram</li><li>Creating Resource Interaction in SV-1 diagram</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575419 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01608: MODAF. SV-10a Resource Constraints Specification

- page_id: `249575419`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575419/MODAF.+SV-10a+Resource+Constraints+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.583+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The purpose the SV-10a Resource Constraints Specification is to specify functional and non-functional constraints on the implementation aspects of the architecture (i.e. the structural and behavioral elements of the SV viewpoint). SV-10a describes constraints on the resources, functions, data and ports that make up the SV physical 
architecture.

The constraints are specified in text and may be functional or structural (i.e. non-functional).

**Implementation**

SV-10a can be represented using:

- A SV-10a table.
- A SV-10a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-10a Resource Constraints Specification

**Related views**

SV-10a describes constraints on resources, functions, and data that make up the SV physical architecture.

Where a Resource Constraint is based on some standard, then that standard should be listed in the Standards Profile (TV-1).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resource Constraint
- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Function
- Entity Item
- Resource Interaction
- Exchange Element

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SV-10a table

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575421 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01609: MODAF. SV-10b Resource State Transition Description

- page_id: `249575421`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575421/MODAF.+SV-10b+Resource+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.618+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Resource State Transition Description (SV-10b) is a graphical method of describing a ResourceType’s (or Function’s) response to various events by changing its state. The diagram basically represents the sets of events to which the subjects will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action.

**Implementation**

SV-10b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-10b Resource State Transition Description

**Related views**

An SV-10a describes constraints on resources, functions, and data that make up the SV physical architecture.

Where a Resource Constraint is based on some standard, then that standard should be listed in the Standards Profile (TV-1).

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resources State Machine
- Resource State
- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The diagram basically represents the sets of events to which the subjects will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SV-10b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sv_10b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575423 space=UPDM2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 01610: MODAF. SV-10c Resource Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `249575423`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575423/MODAF.+SV-10c+Resource+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T16:01:34.885+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Resource Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the interactions between functional resources. Each event-trace diagram will have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation. The SV-10c is valuable for moving to the next level of detail from the initial solution design, to help define a sequence of functions and system data interfaces, and to ensure that each participating Resource or System Port role has the necessary information it needs, at the right time, in order to perform its assigned functionality.

**Implementation**

SV-10c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-10c Resource Event-Trace Description

You can also create SV-10c Business Process Diagram (BPD). In order to do that, use the Cameo Business Modeler plugin. For more information on how to model the business process diagram, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='BPMN Process Diagram' space='MT'].

**Related views**

The SV-10c is typically used in conjunction with the Resource State Transition Description (SV-10b) to describe the dynamic behavior of resources.

The data content of ‘messages’ that connect life-lines in an SV-10c View Product may be related, in modelling terms, with resource interactions (SV-1, 3), data flows (SV-4, 6) and data schema entities (SV-11) modeled in other views.

- Resource Event Trace
- Resource Message
- Resource Role
- Resource Interaction
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel
- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post

- Resource Interaction Creation Wizard
- Resource Interaction Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Each event-trace diagram will have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation. The SV-10c is valuable for moving to the next level of detail from the initial solution design, to help define a sequence of functions and system data interfaces, and to ensure that each participating Resource or System Port role has the necessary information it needs, at the right time, in order to perform its assigned functionality.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SV-10c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sv_10c.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>SV-10c Resource Event-Trace Description</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3b2bf44-2cb8-4aac-bc12-7bedd183a64f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also create SV-10c Business Process Diagram (BPD). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575425 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01611: MODAF. SV-11 Physical Schema

- page_id: `249575425`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575425/MODAF.+SV-11+Physical+Schema
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.691+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The SV-11 View defines the structure of the various kinds of system data that are utilized by the systems in the Architecture. The Physical Schema is one of the Architectural Products closest to actual system design in the Framework. SV-11 is used to describe how the information represented in the Information Model (OV-7) is actually implemented. While the mapping between the logical and physical data models is relatively straightforward, the relationship between the components of each model (e.g. entity types in the logical model versus relational tables in the physical model) is frequently one-to-many or many-to-many.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in SV-11. It supports the information engineering notation within this view.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in SV-11. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

SV-11 can be represented using:

- A SV-11 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-11 Physical Schema

**Related views**

The Physical Schema is one of the Architectural Products closest to actual system design in the Framework. An SV-11 is used to describe how information represented in the Information Model (OV-7) is actually implemented.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Entity Item
- Entity Attribute
- Entity Relationship
- Exchange Element
- Details
- Physical Data Model

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575427 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01612: MODAF. SV-12 Service Provision

- page_id: `249575427`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575427/MODAF.+SV-12+Service+Provision
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.729+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The SV-12 defines the relationships between the Capability Configurations and Services.

**Implementation**

SV-12 can be represented using a SV-12 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements and the Resources will be used as the column elements.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-12 Service Provision

**Related views**

The service attributes defined in SOV-1 can be given values in an SV-12 and related to the environment under which those values are true.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Service Interface
- Service
- Request

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Building SV-12 Service Provision matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The SV-12 defines the relationships between the Capability Configurations and Services.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SV-12 can be represented using a SV-12 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements and the Resources will be used as the column elements.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sv_12.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P" ri:content-title="MODAF. SV-12 Service Provision" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SV-12 Service Provision</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The service attributes defined in SOV-1 can be given values in an SV-12 and related to the environment under which those values are true.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61da4b10-2c98-45ac-b095-43156cad56a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Resource Artifact</li><li>Software</li><li>Capability Configuration</li><li>Organization</li><li>Post</li><li>Service Interface</li><li>Service</li><li>Request</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c80e1447-6ee2-43c3-8956-9146ba5a6e4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedures</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Building SV-12 Service Provision matrix</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575397 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01613: MODAF. SV-2 Resource Communications Description

- page_id: `249575397`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575397/MODAF.+SV-2+Resource+Communications+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.192+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Systems Communications Description series of views specifies the communications networks and pathways 
that link systems, and provides details regarding their configuration. The networks and pathways documented 
through these views represent the physical implementation of the information needlines identified in an 
Operational Node Connectivity Description (OV-2). The SV-2 series focuses on the physical characteristics of 
each link, to include specification of such attributes as the geographic location of network components (e.g. 
routers, switches, amplifiers and repeaters). Attributes such as capacities (e.g. bandwidth, throughput), 
frequencies used, security encryption methods used, and other descriptive information are usually presented in a 
corresponding SV-6 product (though most architecture tools would prompt the architect to enter such data as the 
SV-2 views are being developed).

**Implementation**

SV-2 can be represented using:

- A SV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- A SV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A UML Class diagram.
- A UML Composite Structure diagram.
- A SysML Block Definition diagram.
- A SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Related views**

Any protocol and Standard referred to in an SV-2 diagram must be defined in the TV-1 Technical Standards View.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Resource Port
- Resource Connector
- Resource Role
- Resource Interface
- Protocol
- Standard
- Service
- Request
- Resource Interaction
- Control
- Exchange Element
- Energy
- Materiel

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Creating SV-2 diagram
- Creating Resource Interaction in SV-2 diagram

#### PANEL: Related references

Related references

- Resource Interaction Creation Wizard
- Resource Interaction Manager Dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The networks and pathways documented<br />through these views represent the physical implementation of the information needlines identified in an<br />Operational Node Connectivity Description (OV-2). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575400 space=UPDM2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 01615: MODAF. SV-3 Resource Interaction Matrix

- page_id: `249575400`
- space_key: `UPDM2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UPDM2P/pages/249575400/MODAF.+SV-3+Resource+Interaction+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-04T11:07:15.240+02:00`
- ancestors: UPDM 2 Plugin Documentation > MODAF > MODAF Systems Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

The Resource Interaction Matrix provides a tabular summary of the resource interactions specified in the SV-1 for the Architecture. An SV-3 Product allows a quick overview of all the resource interactions specified in one or more SV-1 diagrams. The SV-3 can be organized in a number of ways to emphasize the association of groups of system pairs in context with the architecture’s purpose.

**Implementation**

SV-3 can be represented using a SV-3 diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. Rows and Columns in the matrix represent the Resources.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SV-3 Resource Interaction Matrix

**Related views**

SV-3 is a summary description of the resource interactions that are identified in SV-1.

#### PANEL: Related elements

Related elements

- Resource Artifact
- Software
- Capability Configuration
- Organization
- Post
- Exchange Element
- Resource Interaction
- Materiel
- Energy

#### PANEL: Related procedures

Related procedures

- Building SV-3 matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
